The onboarding server — the brain, the voice, the tools

The nexus-side of the app: the HTTP surface the client calls, as a server block. Every route the onboarding conversation drives — the plan brain's turns, the web search + agent bash tools, the designer's picks, the personality + voice catalogs, the voice-engine toggle — declared as routes with typed handlers that return JSON. Placement is the point: this runs on the BEAM/nexus, not the browser. It leans on the app's modules (PlanBrain, Requisition, VoiceEngine, PlanTools, Nexus.Browse) — the logic; this block is the door.

The performer — voice, face, hands, deck

The stage engine: it adopts the shared #self-cube, drives the audio-driven mouth (visemes), the gaze (holding the owner's eyes while they talk), the push-to-talk capture with live transcription + acknowledgment nods, the personality motion engine (idle drift, the speaking bob, GoEmotions activations), the streaming Kokoro TTS player, the custom slide deck, and the signature moves. This is the whole body-in-motion — our code; the STT/VAD libraries it loads are vendored assets.

The onboarding surfaces

The get-to-know-you experience woven around the cube: the personality/voice/ color/shape designer, the requisition pairing, the live plan conversation (composer state machine, push-to-talk, thought bubble), the animated slide deck, and the palette/shape vocabulary. Each was a numbered browser script; here each is a client block sharing the page's global scope exactly as before — our onboarding code, one literate surface.

The avatar's body — the 3D cube

The self-cube's physical body: a real WebGL (Three.js) extruded-squircle mesh with a matte matcap finish (white core, the character's colour pooling in the depth), an inverted-hull toon outline, capsule hands, and the four shapes — squircle, round, blocky, and the organic blob (a bounded, seamless vertex wobble). It mounts onto #self-cube and reads the CSS rotation vars the performer writes. Three.js is a vendored WASM/JS dependency, referenced as an asset; this block is our body.

The shell — the app's DOM, chrome, and boot

The real desktop shell (was app.html): the frameless window chrome, the #app root the vault/graph/onboarding mount into, the shared #self-cube stage, and the boot. The CSS is now [[design]] blocks and every hand-authored script is a woven client block, so this carries only the DOM, the true vendor libraries (d3/lucide/gsap/lottie/intake — vendored assets), and the token the serve layer injects. __TOKEN__/__CHROME__ are substituted at serve time.

    autopoet
    /ˌɔː·toʊ·ˈpoʊ·ɪt/ · noun · poiesis

    A self-authoring system — it keeps a living body of structure it writes and rewrites from your notes, so your plain words become real, running form. It reads what you mean, and makes what it means.

    // autopoiesis — the making that makes itself
    a free account — your agent's identity, credits & cloud

    The rest of the app's browser code: the vault tree, the world graph, the composer, node details, badges, popups, the save/prompt flow, the nexus/ inference choice screens, and the onboarding quiz. Each was a numbered script; here each is a client block sharing global scope — ported faithfully.

    The design system

    Every stylesheet the app wears, as design blocks — one per concern, ported faithfully from the app's look. The shell tokens live in the app root; these dress the surfaces that compose around the cube: the plan conversation, the designer, the deck, the call bar, the sidebar, the onboarding gate, and the world graph.

    part0

    vault tree green in the mock

    prose sketch editor blue in the mock

    the world graph

    new in vault modal ours in theme lig

    the save prompt command only now liv

    voice a meet style call bar floating

    customize autopoet editor cube cente

    canvas badges top right search notif

    sidebar footer account settings env

    onboarding sign in a pre app screen

    interactive plan mode the bottom nex

    the animated slide deck gsap face ca

    nexus choice

    requisition

    Autopoet — your onboarding companion

    Autopoet is the desktop app where a new owner meets their autopoet: a 3D cube avatar that greets them, gets to know them, and sets up their whole nexus environment — workspace, agents, rules, integrations, cadence — while drafting the plan as a deck. This is that app, authored entirely as .work.

    Everything here is one literate tree: prose narrates, design blocks carry the look, client blocks are the browser UI (the grid-paper world, the 3D cube, the onboarding conversation, the deck), and server blocks are the brain + voice on the nexus. Woven by work, served by Nexus.Server, shipped as the Elixir desktop app. No stray .js, no hand-authored .html — the workbook IS the app.

    The world

    The avatar's world is grid paper — light or ink per theme. The cube performs on it; the graph layer reveals behind it; the onboarding surfaces (the pinned question, the deck, the designer) compose around it. These tokens + the stage geometry are the whole visual foundation every other surface builds on.
